bytes.topic: Project Setup & Config — zero-config vs explicit config, config file structure
bytes.title: esbuild Config — Zero-Config Convenience vs Explicit Control
bytes.body:
esbuild has no config file convention — there is no esbuild.config.js that it discovers automatically. Every invocation is explicit: CLI flags or a JavaScript/TypeScript build script. The "zero-config" experience comes from sane defaults (no transforms unless asked, no bundling unless --bundle is passed), not from file discovery.

For anything beyond a one-liner, a build script calling the esbuild JS API is the idiomatic pattern. You get full Node.js expressiveness: conditional logic, environment variable injection via `define`, multiple entry points, and plugin composition. The `build()` call is synchronous in intent but returns a Promise — always await it and inspect the `metafile` if you need bundle analysis.

Key config knobs developers miss: `splitting: true` requires `format: 'esm'`; `sourcemap: 'external'` writes a separate `.map` instead of inlining; `target` accepts browser version arrays like `['chrome120', 'firefox115']` — not just ES year strings. Skipping explicit `platform` defaults to `browser`, which stubs Node built-ins silently.

bytes.topic: Dev Server — HMR, proxy setup, HTTPS in dev
bytes.title: esbuild Dev Server — Live Reload, Reverse Proxy, and HTTPS Without a Plugin
bytes.body:
esbuild's built-in dev server, available via `context().serve()`, handles static file serving and incremental rebuilds out of the box — but true HMR isn't included. Instead, esbuild exposes a Server-Sent Events endpoint at `/__esbuild` that emits `change` events on every rebuild. Inject a small SSE listener into your entry point and call `location.reload()` to wire up live reload yourself.

For HTTPS, pass `certfile` and `keyfile` directly to `serve()`. Pair with `mkcert` to generate trusted local certs in seconds.

Proxy support isn't built-in, but `serve()` accepts an `onRequest` callback where you can forward unmatched requests (e.g., `/api/*`) to a backend using Node's `http.request`. This avoids CORS entirely during development. The `host` option lets you bind to `0.0.0.0` for LAN device testing. Combine `ctx.watch()` with `ctx.serve()` — watch triggers rebuilds, serve delivers the updated bundle. Never call `ctx.rebuild()` manually in dev; watch handles invalidation.

bytes.topic: Code Splitting — manual chunks, dynamic imports, route-based splitting
bytes.title: esbuild Code Splitting — Manual Chunks, Dynamic Imports, and Route-Based Splitting
bytes.body:
esbuild's code splitting requires two things: `format: 'esm'` and `splitting: true`. CJS output silently ignores the flag — ESM is non-negotiable. You'll also need `outdir` instead of `outfile`.

Splitting works two ways. Dynamic imports are the primary driver — any `import('...')` call causes esbuild to split the target into its own chunk, respecting the full dependency graph. Modules imported by multiple chunks are hoisted into shared chunks automatically, named by content hash unless you override with `chunkNames`.

Multiple entry points trigger manual chunking: pass an object to `entryPoints` with logical names as keys. Shared code across entries lands in auto-generated chunks — this is route-based splitting without a framework. Tree-shaking still runs per-chunk, so lazy-loaded routes won't carry dead imports from your main bundle.

One gotcha: circular ESM imports across chunk boundaries can cause runtime errors that don't surface at build time. Set `metafile: true` and inspect `outputs[entry].imports` to map chunk relationships before shipping to production.

bytes.topic: Tree Shaking — how it works, why side effects matter, marking packages as pure
bytes.title: esbuild Tree Shaking — Side Effects, Pure Annotations, and Dead Code Elimination
bytes.body:
esbuild tree-shakes ESM bundles by default — unused exports are dropped during bundling, not as a separate pass. The critical lever is side-effect analysis. If esbuild can't prove a module import is side-effect-free, it keeps the entire module even if nothing is consumed from it.

Two mechanisms control this. First, `sideEffects: false` in a package's `package.json` tells esbuild the package performs no top-level side effects, making entire files eligible for removal when their exports go unused. Without it, a barrel file like `export * from './utils'` forces retention of every re-export, even unused ones.

Second, the `pure` API option (or the inline `/* @__PURE__ */` comment) marks specific function calls as safe to eliminate when their return value is unused. This is essential for class factories and decorator wrappers that esbuild would otherwise conservatively retain.

esbuild's tree shaking only works on ESM source. CommonJS modules are opaque — esbuild cannot statically analyze `require()` call sites, so CJS imports are never tree-shaken. Confirm your entry points and dependencies output ESM to unlock dead code elimination.

bytes.topic: Bundle Analysis — visualizing size, finding bloat, setting size budgets in CI
bytes.title: esbuild Bundle Analysis — Visualizing Size, Finding Bloat, and Enforcing Size Budgets in CI
bytes.body:
esbuild's `--metafile` flag emits a JSON artifact containing every module's byte size, import graph, and chunk membership. Feed that file into `esbuild.analyzeMetafile()` to get a tree-printed breakdown in your terminal, or pipe it to the official bundle visualizer at esbuild.github.io/analyze for an interactive treemap. To catch bloat automatically, read the metafile in your build script and assert against `outputs[file].bytes`. If any output chunk exceeds your budget, exit with a non-zero code — CI fails, the PR is blocked.

The metafile also exposes `inputs`, letting you trace exactly which source files contribute to a given output. This is invaluable for diagnosing why a utility-only import like `lodash-es` ballooned a chunk: follow the `imports` array to find the transitive culprit. Pair `--bundle --splitting --chunk-names=[hash]` with per-chunk budgets to enforce granular limits on lazy-loaded routes, not just the total bundle.

bytes.topic: Asset Handling — images, fonts, SVGs, hashing for cache busting
bytes.title: esbuild Asset Handling — Loaders, Hashing, and Cache Busting
bytes.body:
esbuild treats assets through its `loader` option — each file extension maps to a strategy. For images and fonts, `file` emits the asset to `outdir` and replaces the import with a URL string. `dataurl` inlines as a base64 data URI, useful for small icons. `base64` gives you the raw string without the URI prefix. SVGs are flexible: use `text` to import markup as a string, `dataurl` to embed inline, or `file` to emit and reference externally.

Cache busting is handled via `assetNames`. The `[hash]` placeholder generates a content hash from the file bytes — deterministic and stable across rebuilds unless the file changes. Pair it with `[dir]/[name]-[hash]` to preserve directory structure. Set `publicPath` to prefix emitted URLs with your CDN origin, so `import logo from './logo.png'` resolves to `https://cdn.example.com/assets/logo-Xk92a.png` at runtime.

For SVG sprite workflows, import SVGs as `text`, concatenate symbols server-side, and emit once — esbuild won't deduplicate identical `file`-loaded assets automatically, so manual bundling still wins there.

bytes.topic: Environment Variables — .env files, build-time vs runtime, define plugin
bytes.title: esbuild Environment Variables — define, .env Files, and Build-Time vs Runtime
bytes.body: esbuild has no built-in .env loader. Unlike Vite or webpack, it won't auto-inject process.env from a .env file — you wire it manually using the define option, which performs a textual find-and-replace at bundle time, not runtime. That distinction matters: define substitutions are inlined as literals before any JS executes, so dead-code elimination kicks in. Set NODE_ENV to 'production' and esbuild will tree-shake every if (process.env.NODE_ENV === 'development') branch out of the bundle entirely. To load .env values, use dotenv to parse the file and spread the result into define, prefixing each key with 'process.env.'. Never pass secrets this way — define values are visible in the output bundle. For values that must stay server-side, keep them out of esbuild's input entirely. For browser targets, esbuild doesn't shim process.env automatically; if you see ReferenceError: process is not defined, add 'process.env.NODE_ENV': JSON.stringify('production') explicitly or inject a global shim via inject.

bytes.topic: Source Maps — types, trade-offs in production
bytes.title: esbuild Source Maps — Types, Trade-offs, and What to Ship to Production
bytes.body:
esbuild supports four source map modes via the `sourcemap` option: `true` (or `"linked"`), `"external"`, `"inline"`, and `"both"`. Each has distinct production implications.

`"linked"` emits a separate `.js.map` file and appends a `//# sourceMappingURL=` comment to the bundle — best for production when you serve maps privately or upload to Sentry/Datadog. `"external"` does the same but omits the URL comment, giving you the file without exposing the reference to end users. `"inline"` base64-encodes the entire map into the bundle — never use this in production; it inflates bundle size by 2–3×. `"both"` combines inline and external, useful only for tooling compatibility.

For esbuild specifically, source maps are generated at near-zero cost due to its columnar AST design — unlike webpack, there's no measurable build-time penalty for enabling them. The real trade-off is exposure: shipping `"linked"` maps publicly leaks your source tree. Use `"external"`, strip the `sourceMappingURL` comment at deploy time, then upload the `.map` files directly to your error tracker using its CLI before deleting them from the artifact.

bytes.topic: TypeScript — transpile-only vs type-check, speed vs safety trade-off
bytes.title: esbuild TypeScript — Transpile-Only vs Type-Check, Speed vs Safety
bytes.body:
esbuild strips TypeScript types without running tsc — it never type-checks. That's why `esbuild src/index.ts --bundle --outfile=dist/out.js` finishes in milliseconds regardless of codebase size. esbuild treats TypeScript as syntactic sugar: it parses, removes annotations, and emits JavaScript. Type errors are invisible to it.

This is intentional. During development, the feedback loop is fast. In CI, you run `tsc --noEmit` separately as a pure type-check pass — no output files, just diagnostics. The two tools do different jobs.

The risk: esbuild will happily emit JavaScript from code that tsc would reject. A mistyped function signature, a missing property, an impossible cast — all become runtime bugs. If you skip `tsc --noEmit` in your pipeline, you have no safety net.

The pattern that works: use esbuild for all emit (dev server, production bundle) and tsc exclusively for type validation. Wire both into your CI job. Never rely on esbuild's output as proof of type correctness — it has no opinion on that.

bytes.topic: CSS Processing — PostCSS, CSS Modules, preprocessor support
bytes.title: esbuild CSS Processing — PostCSS, CSS Modules, and Preprocessor Support Explained
bytes.body: esbuild handles CSS natively but draws a hard line at PostCSS and preprocessors: it has no built-in support for either. For Sass/Less, you must pre-compile to plain CSS before passing files to esbuild — pipe `sass` output directly into esbuild's `stdin` using the `loader: { '.css': 'css' }` option, or run it as a separate step. CSS Modules are supported via `loader: { '.module.css': 'local-css' }` (added in v0.17). esbuild rewrites class names to unique identifiers and exposes them as a JS object via the default export. PostCSS requires an esbuild plugin — the `esbuild-postcss` or `esbuild-style-plugin` packages wrap PostCSS in an `onLoad` hook, intercept `.css` files, run the PostCSS pipeline, and return transformed CSS to esbuild. For autoprefixer or Tailwind v3 (which uses PostCSS internally), this plugin approach is mandatory. esbuild's own CSS bundler handles `@import` inlining and URL remapping natively — lean on that before reaching for plugins.

bytes.topic: Aliasing & Path Resolution — @ aliases, module resolution config
bytes.title: esbuild Aliasing & Path Resolution — Native alias vs. onResolve Plugins
bytes.body:
esbuild's built-in `alias` option handles exact package-name substitution — ideal for swapping `react` with `preact/compat` or mocking heavy libs in tests. Pass it in `buildOptions` as a plain object: `alias: { 'react': 'preact/compat' }`. Resolution is exact-match only; it won't expand prefix patterns.

For TypeScript-style `@/` path aliases, `alias` falls short. esbuild has no native tsconfig `paths` resolver. Instead, write a small plugin using `build.onResolve`. Filter on a regex like `/^@\//`, strip the prefix, and return an absolute path via `path.resolve`. This runs at bundle time with zero runtime cost.

Two gotchas: First, `alias` entries take precedence over `node_modules` — use this intentionally to polyfill browser globals. Second, `onResolve` callbacks receive the importer's directory in `args.resolveDir`; use it when resolving relative-to-importer paths, not a hardcoded project root. Pair your plugin's resolved path with `namespace: 'file'` to keep esbuild's default file loader chain intact.

bytes.topic: Performance — build speed, caching, parallel processing, incremental builds
bytes.title: esbuild's Context API — Stop Paying the Cold-Start Tax on Every Rebuild
bytes.body:
esbuild's speed comes from three sources: Go's native compilation, aggressive parallelism across all CPU cores, and a parse-once AST cache shared across rebuilds. For dev workflows where you're triggering builds repeatedly, the Context API (`esbuild.context()`) is what keeps that cache alive between invocations.

A `context()` call returns a long-lived build context. Calling `ctx.rebuild()` reuses the parsed module graph from the previous build — only re-transforming files whose content changed. This is fundamentally different from calling `esbuild.build()` in a loop, which discards all cached state and starts cold every time.

`ctx.watch()` wraps `rebuild()` with esbuild's built-in file watcher, debouncing changes and surfacing errors without crashing the process. For CI pipelines or test runners where you control the trigger, prefer `ctx.rebuild()` directly — it returns a `BuildResult` you can inspect programmatically, including `errors`, `warnings`, and optionally a `metafile` for bundle analysis.

One sharp edge: always call `ctx.dispose()` when finished. A leaked context holds open OS-level file watchers and prevents the Node.js process from exiting.

bytes.topic: Production Optimization — minification, compression (gzip/brotli), dead code elimination
bytes.title: esbuild Production Optimization — Minification, Tree-Shaking, and Brotli in One Pass
bytes.body:
esbuild's `minify: true` is shorthand for three distinct passes: `minifyWhitespace` strips comments and formatting, `minifyIdentifiers` renames local variables to single characters, and `minifySyntax` collapses constant expressions and simplifies control flow. Use them individually in development for targeted profiling, together in production.

Tree-shaking is automatic for ESM (`format: 'esm'`), but you can force it on CommonJS output with `treeShaking: true`. Mark library packages with `"sideEffects": false` in their `package.json` to let esbuild drop unused exports aggressively — without it, esbuild conservatively retains entire modules.

esbuild has no built-in gzip/brotli writer, but setting `write: false` captures `outputFiles` as `Uint8Array` buffers in memory. Pipe them through Node's `zlib.gzipSync` and `zlib.brotliCompressSync` to write compressed variants alongside the originals. Enable `metafile: true` and call `esbuild.analyzeMetafile()` to verify dead-code removal is actually working — unexpectedly large chunks almost always trace back to a missing `sideEffects` annotation or a dynamic `require()` call defeating static analysis.

bytes.topic: Plugin System — how plugins extend the build pipeline
bytes.title: esbuild Plugin System — Intercepting the Build Pipeline with Hooks
bytes.body:
esbuild's plugin API exposes two primary hooks: `onResolve` and `onLoad`. Every plugin is an object with a `name` and a `setup(build)` function — that's the entire contract.

`onResolve` intercepts module resolution. You match against an import path using a `filter` regex and optionally a `namespace`, then return a custom `path` and `namespace`. This lets you redirect imports to virtual modules or alternative file paths without touching disk.

`onLoad` intercepts file reading. Match by namespace (and optionally path), then return `contents` and a `loader` (`js`, `ts`, `css`, `json`, etc.). esbuild skips the filesystem entirely and uses what you return.

The `filter` regex runs in Go — keep it simple and avoid lookaheads. Plugins run sequentially; the first matching hook wins. Use `build.onStart` and `build.onEnd` for lifecycle work like cache invalidation or writing manifests.

All hooks are async-capable: return a Promise and esbuild awaits it. Plugins compose cleanly — pass them as an array to `plugins` in your `BuildOptions`.

bytes.topic: Vite-specific — native ESM in dev, esbuild pre-bundling, Rollup in prod
bytes.title: esbuild in Vite — Pre-Bundling Deps in Dev While Rollup Takes Production
bytes.body:
Vite uses esbuild for exactly two jobs in dev: transforming TypeScript/JSX (type-stripping only, no type-checking) and pre-bundling dependencies. Pre-bundling converts CJS/UMD packages to ESM and coalesces multi-file packages — lodash-es ships 600+ files — into a single cached module at node_modules/.vite/deps. Without this, the browser would fire hundreds of cascading HTTP requests before your page renders a single component.

The cache is keyed to your lockfile hash and reruns only on dep changes. Force a rebuild with `vite --force` or by deleting node_modules/.vite manually.

In production, Vite swaps esbuild for Rollup, which has more mature support for advanced code splitting, deterministic chunk hashing, and tree-shaking across dynamic imports. But esbuild stays available as the prod minifier via `build.minify: 'esbuild'` — 20–40x faster than Terser with negligible output size difference.

Customize pre-bundling through `optimizeDeps.esbuildOptions`, which accepts the full esbuild build API surface: loaders, define substitutions, and esbuild plugins. The `esbuild` top-level key separately controls the transform step for your source files.

bytes.topic: Webpack-specific — loaders vs plugins, module federation
bytes.title: esbuild's Take on Webpack Loaders, Plugins, and the Module Federation Gap
bytes.body:
esbuild draws a hard line between loaders and plugins—Webpack blurs it. Loaders in esbuild are built-in, Go-native file-type handlers declared in the build options: `{ '.svg': 'text', '.png': 'dataurl', '.wasm': 'binary' }`. They run at near-zero cost because they never leave the Go runtime. That's your Webpack `file-loader`, `url-loader`, and `raw-loader` collapsed into a single config key.

Plugins fill the gap when builtins can't. The plugin API exposes two hooks: `onResolve` (intercept module resolution) and `onLoad` (transform file content). An `onLoad` callback returning `{ contents, loader: 'jsx' }` is the direct equivalent of a Webpack loader chain—but without chaining. In esbuild, the first `onLoad` that matches a filter wins; there's no pipe.

Module federation is where esbuild hits a wall. There is no `ModuleFederationPlugin` counterpart. The pragmatic escape hatch: expose micro-frontend remotes as self-contained ESM bundles served from a CDN, then consume them at runtime with dynamic `import('https://remote/entry.js')`. This trades build-time federation for runtime composition—esbuild builds each remote independently, no shared plugin contract required.

bytes.topic: Monorepo Support — workspace packages, shared configs, turborepo integration
bytes.title: esbuild in a Monorepo — Workspace Packages, Shared Configs, and Turborepo Caching
bytes.body:
esbuild has no native workspace awareness — you wire it up explicitly. The core pattern: set `packages: 'external'` to prevent bundling workspace siblings, then use `alias` to redirect package names to their local source or dist paths, avoiding redundant bundle passes.

For shared build configuration, export a base `BuildOptions` object from a dedicated tooling package and spread it in each package's build script. esbuild's JS API accepts plain objects, so composition is zero-cost and type-safe.

Turborepo integration is straightforward: declare a `build` task in `turbo.json` with `outputs: ["dist/**"]` and `dependsOn: ["^build"]` so upstream packages build first. Turbo's content-hash caching means unchanged packages skip esbuild entirely — the biggest win in large repos.

For watch mode, prefer esbuild's `context.watch()` JS API over the CLI flag. Instantiate one `BuildContext` per package in a root orchestrator script, sharing a single plugin registry across all contexts. This avoids spawning N separate processes.

One consistent gotcha: `tsconfig.paths` in a workspace root don't auto-resolve. Use explicit `alias` entries or `esbuild-plugin-tsconfig-paths` — esbuild won't infer them from `tsconfig.json` automatically.

bytes.topic: Dev Tooling — config sharing, extending base configs, CI build caching
bytes.title: esbuild Config Sharing — Base Configs, Overrides, and CI Caching That Actually Works
bytes.body:
esbuild has no built-in config file format — your config is just a JS/TS module exporting a `BuildOptions` object. That's your sharing primitive. Define a `base.config.ts` that exports a `BuildOptions`, then spread it per environment: `{ ...baseConfig, minify: true, sourcemap: false }` for prod, `{ ...baseConfig, sourcemap: 'inline' }` for dev. For monorepos, publish the base as a local package or use a path alias — esbuild doesn't care, it's just an import.

For CI caching, esbuild's cache lives in the OS temp dir by default and isn't portable. The real win is caching your `node_modules` (esbuild's native binary lives there) and using `--metafile` output to detect what actually changed. Pair `--metafile=meta.json` with a script that diffs input hashes between runs — skip rebuilds entirely for unchanged entry points. esbuild's incremental builds (`incremental: true` + `rebuild()`) persist in-process only, so they're useless in CI. Lean on artifact caching (`actions/cache` keyed on lockfile hash) and split entry points so only dirty chunks rebuild.

bytes.topic: Monitoring — build time tracking, bundle size regression alerts in CI
bytes.title: esbuild Monitoring — Build Time Tracking and Bundle Size Regression Alerts in CI
bytes.body:
esbuild exposes bundle metadata through the `metafile` build option. Enable it and `result.metafile.outputs` maps every output file to its `bytes`, `inputs`, and full import graph — no extra plugin required. esbuild doesn't surface timing internally, so wrap `esbuild.build()` with `performance.now()` from Node's `perf_hooks`; since esbuild executes synchronously via its Go core, the measurement is clean with no async scheduling noise.

For CI regression gating, commit a `bundle-baseline.json` with your accepted `bytes` and `ms` thresholds. After each build, compute the delta: if `(newBytes - baseline.bytes) / baseline.bytes > 0.05`, fail the pipeline with `process.exit(1)`. Pipe `esbuild.analyzeMetafile(result.metafile)` output to a GitHub Actions step summary for PR-level visibility — it renders a tree showing exactly which inputs account for which byte counts.

Update the baseline file on main only for intentional size increases, keeping the diff reviewable. This gives you sub-second CI feedback without a separate Webpack Bundle Analyzer or third-party service.
